speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the friend that you met on the train. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Sara Gibstayn. And when I saw her, I can't express the reaction what I had. I mean, here I was from such a fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, knowing that your whole family and everybody around you is dead-- and there, I find a friend. But we couldn't really show anything that we know each other. Because we were afraid that, you know, they'll think something-- they'll wonder where? How? So we were sort of staying apart. But I knew that she was there. And that was a great, great relief for me. Did she end up being in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same city. But of course, in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was still '42. It was still the fall, '42. And she was sent in another place to work. And I was in this place. And of course, we had Sundays off. So we visited, once in a while, each other. And-- and I'm telling-- I think I couldn't have survived all this. But she gave me, and I gave her, strength. And to be able to have somebody that close, and to be able to talk to, was-- that's exactly what we needed. Because to keep a secret like that in you, you know, that nothing happened, when so much happened, it was not an easy thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with this family for-- till Christmas. This friend, Sara-- now she's-- can I say what they call her now? They call her Sonya Fox.

speaker: interviewee
text: She got married, of course. And she has children. She lives in Albuquerque, New Mexico.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she knew about another friend that did the same thing. And she was in-- in Austria. She had, even, the address.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wrote to this other friend, and I told her about myself. And she kept on, she wants me to come to Austria. She can't live without me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were with so much pain, it's very-- you can't really tell how-- what you felt. She was also alone. So I said-- so I asked the people that I-- that I was working for, and I told them-- I didn't tell them that I was Jewish, or she was Jewish. But I said, I have a friend that lives in Austria, and would they let me go to visit her for a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also paid a sort of an honorarium-- you know, not much-- but enough to buy a ticket to go on the train to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of currency did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marks, German marks, was

speaker: interviewer
text: So they agreed. They said, yeah. They were very nice people.

speaker: interviewee
text: She even gave me cookies on the way, because it was Christmas. And this was ordinary. She had all sorts of cookies. She gave me cookies to take with me. And I promised I'll come back. I got on this train.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't believe it's me doing all this. Because first of all, I came from a small town-- I didn't-- I wasn't all over the world, traveling and knowing what to do, and how to go. What do I know about Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about Vienna, but I didn't know nothing about it. When I came in Vienna in this huge railroad station, I was lost. I didn't know. And I picked up-- I knew German, what I took in school. Then, when I was with this family, they spoke German. So I picked up quite a bit of German. It was easy for me. But when I came to Austria, I could not understand a word there. Their dialect is so different. It seems like a different language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't hear quite well where they're announcing the trains, and this and that. And I didn't know what to do. I was lost and scared. What if a Gestapo notices me? Did you have to show your identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they asked you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't. I wasn't stopped. And I wasn't asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, so I was sitting forlorn. And there was this one young girl, an Austrian girl. She's saw-- I think-- me, and she-- she said, can I help you? And I-- and-- you know, and I started telling her the story, that I'm going to Kirchberg, and I don't know which train to connect with. And I don't understand the language, it's very hard for me because I've never spoken Austrian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she was like-- I don't know what-- what it was called in German, but she was something like a Girl Guide, that they help out people when they see somebody in need. And she really helped me out. She took me to exactly where I had to go to the train. I got on the train. And I got somewhere in the middle of the night, to Kirchberg. That was the destination,

speaker: interviewer
text: She helped you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And on that train, there were two [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] French prisoners of war. Just me and them== that was all the train carried. Because it was a very small town. And the-- and the station was so far from the town. And as the name says, Kirchberg means-- berg means hills, and kirche is a kirche, is a church.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there are lots of churches in this town. And this town is on a hill. You have to walk up a very steep hill to get to this town. So those-- so we got off the train, me and them. And that's it. So it was the middle of the night. It was dark, and I was frightened. I was such a scared person, I think. So they sort of said to me, whatever German they could muster, to come with them. And they are going also to Kirchberg, and to walk with them. And I did. And I came-- and I walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when we got to the town, the-- the lodges, what they were in, were sort of outside of the town. And I had to go into the town to get to the location where she was at.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked. I don't know how I got there. But all of a sudden, I was in the front of the house. And it was, like, in the middle of the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I was frightened to knock on the door. But I knew that she sleeps-- she told me from the letter-- she sleeps in the basement. So I rapped on the window. And sure enough, she was there. And I mean, she sort of expected me, but she didn't know when. And she came. And I'm telling you, that reunion, I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name, at that time was Golde-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wait a minute. Oy-- Uster. But she ended up in Israel. And her name was Zahava Perchik. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so, I mean, with this other girl, I was friendly. Because in the same little town, you know everybody. But this was my school chum, my good friend, and everything else. And we had, that night, of course, the reunion. We didn't sleep and everything else. But the people that she was with were very nice people too, Austrians. They helped her a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know her identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was a no-no for nobody to know. But I must tell you, that she was-- I was blonde and blue eyed. She was dark, dark hair, with slanted black eyes. She almost looked Chinese or Japanese. But she colored her hair, and it came out bright red. But anyway, she-- she talked her way out of things and into things. So she was-- they had a whole bunch of little kids in that house. And they were a nice couple. And he was-- what do you call-- he cleaned the chimneys. A chimney sweep, the man-- that was his profession. But he kept a nice house, and those children. And she was there. And this Golde, Zahava, she never did a thing in their home. I never did much, either. But I sort of the type that I pick up. But she didn't want to know how to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: They eat a lot of potatoes there. Because they were in Germany, where things weren't so great either. So she-- when she washed the dishes, she couldn't wash off the potatoes from the spoons or forks. And they always had something about it, like, you know, reminding her that she doesn't do the job well. She said, when did I wash dishes? Did you stay in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few days, yes. They let me stay, like a visitor. I came as a visitor and they let me stay there. And then we went to the arbetsrum. Golde said, I'm not going to let you go. You're staying here. I'll arrange everything. And she did. We went to the arbetsrum in the-- in the-- near town. And they-- and she told a story-- I can't remember exactly how the story went. But it was a story, and I want to stay, now, here. I don't want to go back to Katowice, to the other place. And in that time, I already revealed that I can speak German. Because before, in Poland, I wasn't going to say that. But here, I felt a little more at ease, I think. I don't know why-- but the Austrians are nice people, a lot of them. And I said, that I know to speak German. And this lady, that was in the arbetsrum, the whole big [NON-ENGLISH], she needed-- she had relatives that are looking for a girl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the girl has to speak German. Otherwise they don't need her. Because they have a specific job for her. So I said, yes, I speak German. And we started talking in German. And she got, probably, taken with me. I don't know why, but that's probably what it was. She thought I'll fill the bill. And she telephoned them, and they came down and they talked to me. And they were most wonderful people you could ever meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was, the man was a cattle buyer. And he bought cattle for the army. And he shipped them. Because this [NON-ENGLISH] was a small agriculture sort of community. And the farmers around there raised cattle-- because they have wonderful grass there, you know, the Alpine meadows. It's in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my job was-- besides doing housework too. Because there wasn't a job that you did every day-- they bought a bicycle for me. They asked me if I know how to ride a bike. I said, yes, of course. They bought a bicycle for me. They were older people already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said-- before-- like, every Sunday, it was-- the way it was done there. Every Sunday, the farmers or the vendors used to come into their house. They had a beautiful house. And he had an office. And he was sitting there in his office. And the vendors would come in and they would sell them this, or sell him so many, so many, so many. Now, the day before they had to ship them to take them to the yard-- you had to go out to all the farmers and give them the information-- when they should come, where, all those things. Because they haven't-- didn't have any phones. I mean, this couple actually had a phone. But the others didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go on that bike, drive around to all those places, and tell them what to do. And people treated me wonderful. I was really, really, really well, all those buyers-- [NON-ENGLISH], they call them, the farmers in Austria. They used to feed me. They used to give me things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the day of the-- of the transport, the man that I'm talking about, their name I know. It was Wurzinger-- Frau and Herr Wurzinger. And the-- he had-- he would-- his right arm, it was amputated. Or he lost it, I think, in a drunken brawl. Because he was a drinker. And so he didn't have a right arm, and couldn't write. So I had to go with him. As the cattle was weighed, they'd call out the pounds and all those things. And I had to write it in for him. So I went with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my sort of official job. But all the other things, I did lots of things there too. But I didn't mind, because they were wonderful people, had a beautiful home. They had a vineyard. They had everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really learned a lot there, too. I learned how to keep house, how to cook, how to bake. I learned that all there, in that-- in that family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to conceal your identity? Or were you kind of got into the role?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It got better as time went on. In the beginning, it was very-- it wasn't hard to conceal, because nobody asked you who you are. But my own feeling was very, very-- you know, it was so strenuous, I had such a bitter thing in me. But you know, as time went on, and when you're young, I think you sort of let things slip by. And besides, I had my friend with me. And there was the big help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I stayed there till, I think, '44, end of 44, I stayed with them. But then the Germans, themselves-- I mean, things got really bad there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out everybody that was in jobs like that, with families. They took them away and sent them to work. But they really need you. Which, in my case, was in a-- in a sanitarium, where the soldiers came for, you know, for rest and recuperation. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they send me there. And that was called, what? Bad Reichenhall. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: They send me there. And also, with this family, with the Wurzingers, I learned how to sew on a sewing machine. They had a sewing machine. And she showed me how. And I picked it up-- it's not a big deal. But I learned how to sew. In this place, they needed people in the laundry and sewing. Like, you know, you had to-- mostly straight seams. And- and there, I met a whole bunch of Ukrainian women and men that worked in this. And it was very nice there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: They treated us as workers, but not especially bad. You know? We had food, we had places to sleep. And it was nice. Who ran this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the German army. It was from the army. There were also French prisoners working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I got to know all the people. And of course, they didn't know who I was-- they all thought I was Ukrainian. And-- and so it went until liberation. So you stayed in that place? You were there until you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were liberated in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in May of '45. The Russian army liberated us. But I mean, it wasn't just they walked in-- I mean, there were battles and they were fighting. And we had to run from one hiding place to the other, to survive. But eventually, it quieted down and we were liberated. And it wasn't so much fun with the Russians, either. They were very vicious, these soldiers. They raped and they burned, and they killed. But to us, they saw us as almost like prisoners. So they wound that back. But only thing they said-- I mean, there was no government or anything-- but they said, you all have to go back, back where you came from. Which means walking from Austria to Poland. And that's exactly what we did. All the group of people that we were there, we had to go back. Because first of all, they didn't know what's going to happen, yet. I mean, they liberated today. Tomorrow, if the Germans have an offensive again, and they throw them out-- anyway, we had to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure. But I think there must've been around 20, because there were children even. Like, there were couples and they had a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? They took him away and detained him. But then they had a child, so it was brought up and it was with us. So I think there were around 20 people. And we started out walking back, wherever back was. So we-- What did you take with you when you were walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. You can't take much. Can't take any-- but first of all, we didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have probably, a pair of shoes. But I just want to intercept something and tell you about these friends I have and what happened to her. They were French-- not soldiers-- prisoners of war. And when they were starting to bomb this place, where we were-- she was in that place too-- when they started bombing, and you know, machine guns and everything else, and it started to be like a war in that place, the French [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners, they decided they're-- and there was one Englishman too-- they decided they are going to run away into the woods, the Vienna woods. Because it wasn't far from there. So she decided she's going to go with them, leaving me here-- and she'll go with them. I didn't want to go. For-- I don't know what for what reason. But I just didn't feel like going with three men into the wood. But she was--she was a strong person. She went with them. And she left me. That's how we separated. And we didn't get together again till 1972 in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were saved as well, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, going back to the journey, we walked. And on the way, we met a lot of soldiers. I met a Jewish soldier. And I didn't want to tell yet, in the front of the other people, because you don't know for sure. But to him, I confess. How did you know he was a Jewish soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me-- and he looked it. Couldn't miss it. And they gave us chocolate, and some things. You know, little things like that. They sort of treated us well. But we were walking. At night, we came into a place, which was Yugoslavia and Austria and Germany meeting, sort of a horn.

speaker: interviewer
text: A border town?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was Yugoslavia already-- it was a small town. And we came into this place. And we went into a house. And a lot of those border towns, the people went away. Because, you know, they didn't know what's going to happen to them. Some are bombed, some are dead. This house was open. Nobody was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were two oxens there, and a wagon. And we had a couple of men with us, too. So they said, they were going to take the oxen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us on the wagon, and we are going in style. We could-- we found, I think, maybe a few things to eat, bits and pieces. And we ate and we drove around. Then the next night, they decided that we are hungry, worse than we need a ride. And they said they're going to kill one of the animals, and we'll have meat. And we'll cook it and we'll eat. And we did that--we did it. [SIGHS] Anyway, I guess making a long story short, we got to a place in Hungary-- yeah, this place was Yugoslavia, Austria and Hungary meets, not German. We got sort of to the border, to Hungary. And this was, for them, they were liberated earlier. So things were starting already to happen there. A train was going. We got on a-- it was, I think, a freight train, something like that. But we all got in this train. And we were going back to Poland. I got very, very sick on that train. So sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was burning up with fever. There was a couple there, a Hungarian couple. I didn't know a word of Hungarian. But they spoke German. So they said to me, you're not going to make it if you don't do something to yourself. And she said, we're getting off in this location. You come up with us. We know there is a monastery in that place. And go there, and they will take care of you. They still didn't know who I was. I didn't tell anybody on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So without-- what else can I do? I did what they said. And I went to the--to the monastery. What was the name of this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, it was before Nagykanizsa. But it was a small little place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knocked on the door. And a priest came to the door. And I told him how sick I was. And he let me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, come in and they'll give me a place to stay and some food. And they did. And that priest started asking me questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are you? What are you? And I thought, well, to a priest, they're not going to kill me, even if they are-- if they are anti-Semite, they're not going to kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell him, so to get his sympathy a little more. And I told him. And he said, oh you know, there are already Jews in town. And tomorrow morning, when you feel better, I'll take you there. And you'll be with your own people. And you're safe-- very nice. And that's what he did. He took me into this town. It's called Nagykanizsa. And there were already Jews that came back from the concentration camps, and from wherever they came. They already set up a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well--

speaker: interviewer
text: An aid organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, something like that. And they were helping people out to find like food, or a place to stay. But when I got there, and I started talking to them, they did not believe me that I'm Jewish. They-- they just didn't want to believe me. But I guess that anybody can come and tell you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, OK, give me a Seder, and I'll read to you. So you read from a Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saw that I'm not telling any jokes. And I-- and they did give me a place to stay with a family, and food. I guess the relief organizations were already working in those days. Because they actually cooked the food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I stayed with? Definitely. And-- but you had to go three times a day. They had a kitchen, and you had to go there and eat there in their kitchen. And-- and I wasn't in a great hurry to run back to Poland. Because I kind of understood that it can't be that good there. Especially I didn't want to go back where I came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this family was a very nice, lovely family. She was left alone with two children. The husband was killed. They were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- you know, I stayed there that whole summer, till late in the fall. But then it came out, sort of a decree in every country, that they couldn't keep all the refugees. I mean, they couldn't feed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to go back to Poland. And that wasn't a joke. So they put us on a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you had to get-- go yourself and you know, and get on it, and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. And I came to a town, which was now Poland. It's called-- it used to be Germany, [NON-ENGLISH], that's why it has the name, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was Poland now. You know, the way they divided it. And I came to this town. And in that town, I already met people from my own town that came from-- some came from Russia, some came from the partisan. Some came from concentration camp. Well, from our town, not too many people were in concentration camp. They just killed them right there and then. They didn't bother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met a family. I met some other people. And they told me that my cousins from Russia are somewhere here. But they didn't know quite where they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then those cousins, they were two. And one was married with a wife and a child. And one was just a man that his wife got killed and his child. But they were in Russia. So they came back. So when I finally met with them, well, I really felt that I am-- I made it. And I want to say something that doesn't--
